title: Routing
description: File-system pages, layouts, dynamic params, AOT chunks and SSG prerender

sections.heading: The file system is the router

blocks.text: app.fsClientRoutes() crawls pages/ at build time. The path on disk is the URL — move a file, move the route. No route config to maintain.

client/pages/
  index.tsx              ← /
  about.tsx              ← /about
  blog/
    index.tsx            ← /blog
    [slug].tsx           ← /blog/:slug
  docs/
    [slug].tsx           ← /docs/:slug
Pages are .vue (Vue engine) or .tsx (React engine), chosen once in the Howl constructor. Registering page routes with no engine throws.
A handful of underscore-prefixed file names are structural rather than routable:

rows: The HTML shell — <head>, scripts, the outermost wrapper. Stays static in the DOM across navigation.
A layout that wraps every page in its directory and below. Layouts stack root → leaf.
Rendered when a request throws. Receives the serialized error via page props (ctx.error).

blocks.text: Layouts compose by nesting: a request to /blog/my-post renders _app → pages/_layout → pages/blog/_layout → the page, each wrapping the next.
Wrap a path segment in square brackets to capture it. The captured value arrives on the page props as params, and in handlers as ctx.params.
The same [param] folder convention applies to API routes under apis/ — see the API Routes page.
A file-name prefix opts a page into client-side navigation and/or build-time prerender. The prefix is stripped from the URL, so pages/jobs/__index.vue mounts at /jobs/.

rows: Dynamic-imports a client chunk — no server round-trip
Prerendered HTML served from the build snapshot
Dynamic-imports a client chunk — no server round-trip

blocks.text: AOT emits a client chunk so navigation is instant and needs no server. SSG also runs the handler at build time and stores the HTML, served straight from the snapshot.

SSG handlers run at build time with an empty context — no req, no cookies, no per-user state. Anything per-user must stay on dynamic SSR. Dynamic params on SSG routes fall through with a warning until getStaticPaths lands.

SSR renders first paint, then the page hydrates into an SPA. Client-side navigation is opt-in: links inside a client-nav boundary — set it once on <body> in _app — are intercepted and swapped without a full reload, while links outside it do a normal browser navigation. SSR routes fetch fresh HTML; AOT/SSG routes mount their prebuilt chunk with no server round-trip.
For programmatic navigation, both engines expose a router module with the same surface — navigate(), back(), forward(), and a useRoute()/useNavigate() hook:

Add client-prefetch alongside client-nav to warm a destination on hover, touch or focus (off by default; it respects the user's data-saver preference). There is no islands system — the whole page is interactive after hydration; use the IS_SERVER / IS_BROWSER guards from @hushkey/howl to branch per environment.
